title: Targeting the Poor: Evidence from a Field Experiment in Indonesia
authors: Vivi Alatas, Abhijit Banerjee, Rema Hanna, Benjamin A. Olken, Julia Tobias
methodology: Randomized controlled trial (RCT) across 640 villages in North Sumatra and South Sulawesi, Indonesia. Villages were randomly assigned to one of three targeting arms: (1) community-based targeting (CBT) via town-hall meetings where residents rank neighbours by poverty; (2) proxy means test (PMT) using household survey data and statistical scoring; (3) a hybrid approach combining PMT lists with community verification meetings.
data_used: Household survey data from approximately 16,000 households across 640 villages in Indonesia (2008). Data collected on consumption, assets, and demographic characteristics to benchmark targeting accuracy.
key_findings: CBT better identifies households that communities consider poor; PMT better tracks per-capita consumption expenditure. CBT has lower administrative costs and higher community legitimacy. The hybrid approach performs well on both metrics. Targeting errors exist under all methods. Elite households are somewhat over-represented in CBT lists but the effect is modest. Community and consumption-based definitions of poverty diverge significantly — raising the normative question of whose poverty definition should govern program eligibility.
gap_addressed: The literature lacked rigorous experimental evidence on which targeting mechanism — CBT or PMT — actually identifies the poor better in practice, and at what cost. Existing comparisons were non-experimental and confounded by program context.
gaps_highlighted: Results are from Indonesia and may not generalise to weaker-state, higher-poverty contexts. The paper does not resolve whether community or consumption-based definitions of poverty are normatively correct. Elite capture mechanisms are not directly tested. Long-run effects of targeting choices on social cohesion are not examined. The study assumes sufficient within-village inequality to make discrimination feasible.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: This is the anchor CBT paper, but its implicit assumption — that meaningful discrimination between poor and non-poor is socially and statistically feasible — breaks down in Mali's Sahel, where near-universal vulnerability means selection ratios are so low that community committees face not an information problem but a social impossibility problem. The 'managing the impossible' dynamic is precisely what this paper's design cannot capture.

title: Does Elite Capture Matter? Local Elites and Targeted Welfare Programs in Indonesia
authors: Vivi Alatas, Abhijit Banerjee, Rema Hanna, Benjamin A. Olken, Ririn Purnamasari, Matthew Wai-Poi
methodology: Combined field experiment and non-experimental analysis. In 400 villages, the researchers experimentally varied the degree of elite influence over targeting decisions for the PKH conditional cash transfer program. The non-experimental component analysed allocation patterns across multiple existing Indonesian transfer programs (PKH, Raskin, BLT) using household survey and administrative data. Elite status was defined both by formal leadership positions and informal community standing.
data_used: Data from 400 villages in Indonesia. Household-level survey data merged with administrative records from multiple transfer programs (PKH, Raskin, BLT). The 2008 PPLS (poverty pre-screening) household census provided consumption benchmarks. Elite identification drew on village leadership rosters and kinship data.
key_findings: Conditional on consumption, there is overall little evidence that elites and their relatives are disproportionately selected as beneficiaries. However, this masks important heterogeneity: formal leaders (e.g. village heads) are more likely to receive benefits, while informal leaders are less likely. Capture occurs at the benefit distribution stage, not the list-making stage. Critically, the welfare losses from elite capture are quantitatively small — eliminating capture entirely would improve program welfare gains by less than 1% — because formal elites are only about 9% richer than non-elites and represent at most 15% of the population.
gap_addressed: Elite capture of CBT programs had been a major theoretical concern but lacked rigorous empirical quantification. The paper tests whether the concern translates into meaningful welfare losses in practice.
gaps_highlighted: Results may not generalise to contexts where elites are substantially wealthier relative to the poor. The study cannot detect subtler forms of capture (e.g. pressure, exclusion of marginalised groups). It does not directly test CBT meeting dynamics. Social pressure from non-elites — a different political economy problem — is not examined. The paper focuses on Indonesia's relatively institutionalised village governance.
relevance_to_mali_cbt: The paper's most striking finding for Mali is that elite capture matters little precisely because elites are barely better off than non-elites in the Indonesian study villages. This logic extends — and intensifies — in Mali's Sahel, where near-universal poverty means there is virtually no consumption gradient between 'elites' and 'the poor' to exploit. The committee dilemma is therefore not classic elite capture but something more structurally intractable: when everyone qualifies on welfare grounds, no legitimate selection principle exists.
